Taking advantage of someone else’s lack of knowledge, ease, or need goes against God’s laws, even if people don’t always blame you for it. People who steal from countries might escape human punishment, but they won’t avoid God’s judgment.

Cheating the government, borrowing money with no plan to pay it back, avoiding fair payments, spending too much money, and relying on charity when you don't really need to—all of these actions go against God’s rules. So does taking more than workers deserve. These commands tell us to work hard, save wisely, and be satisfied. They also teach us to treat others’ possessions as we want them to treat ours.

The ninth command stops us from saying bad things about anyone. It tells us not to lie, trick, or try to fool others. It also warns against talking badly about someone to damage their reputation. Telling lies in court or spreading rumors and gossip hurts others. Making mistakes sound worse than they are, or trying to boost yourself by tearing down others—these are all forbidden.

How often do we break this rule? All kinds of people do it daily. 

The tenth command stops us from wanting what doesn’t belong to us. Other commands keep us from harming others by our actions. But this one keeps us from desiring what belongs to others just to please ourselves.

20.o: This law, which is so extensive that we cannot measure it, so spiritual that we cannot evade it, and so reasonable that we cannot find fault with it, will be the rule of the future judgment of God, as it is for the present conduct of man. If tried by this rule, we shall find our lives have been passed in transgressions. And with this holy law and an awful judgment before us, who can despise the gospel of Christ? And the knowledge of the law shows our need of repentance. In every believer's heart sin is dethroned and crucified, the law of God is written, and the image of God renewed. The Holy Spirit enables him to hate sin and flee from it, to love and keep this law in sincerity and truth; nor will he cease to repent.
20.m:
This law is so big that we can't fully understand it, so deep that we can't avoid it, and so fair that we can't complain about it. It will be used to judge people in the future just like it guides us now. When we look at our lives through this law, we see that we've broken it many times. 

With this perfect law and the scary thought of being judged, who would want to ignore Jesus' message? Knowing the law makes us realize we need to turn away from our sins. For those who believe, their hearts reject sin, follow God’s laws, and try to become more like Him again. The Holy Spirit helps them hate sin, run away from it, and truly love and obey God's rules. They always keep on feeling sorry for their wrongs.

20.o: Moses having entered into the thick darkness, God there spake in his hearing all that follows from hence to the end of chap. 23, which is mostly an exposition of the ten commandments. The laws in these verses relate to God's worship. The Israelites are assured of God's gracious acceptance of their devotions. Under the gospel, men are encouraged to pray every where, and wherever God's people meet in his name to worship him, he will be in the midst of them; there he will come unto them, and will bless them.
20.m:
Moses went into the dark cloud, and God spoke to him about everything from here until the end of chapter 23. This includes explaining the Ten Commandments. 

The rules in these verses are about worshipping God. God promises to accept the prayers of the Israelites. 

In the time of the gospel, people are told to pray everywhere. Wherever God’s people gather to worship him, he will be there too. He will come to them and bless them.

21.o: The laws in this chapter relate to the fifth and sixth commandments; and though they differ from our times and customs, nor are they binding on us, yet they explain the moral law, and the rules of natural justice. The servant, in the state of servitude, was an emblem of that state of bondage to sin, Satan, and the law, which man is brought into by robbing God of his glory, by the transgression of his precepts. Likewise in being made free, he was an emblem of that liberty wherewith Christ, the Son of God, makes free from bondage his people, who are free indeed; and made so freely, without money and without price, of free grace.
21.m:
The laws in this chapter deal with the fifth and sixth commandments. Even if these laws aren't part of our current rules, they help explain the moral law and what is fair and just.

A servant in slavery was like someone trapped by sin, Satan, and the law because they took away God’s honor by breaking his commands. 

On the other hand, setting a servant free showed how Jesus, the Son of God, frees people from their captivity. He sets them free for free, out of pure grace.

21.o: God, who by his providence gives and maintains life, by his law protects it. A wilful murderer shall be taken even from God's altar. But God provided cities of refuge to protect those whose unhappiness it was, and not their fault, to cause the death of another; for such as by accident, when a man is doing a lawful act, without intent of hurt, happens to kill another. Let children hear the sentence of God's word upon the ungrateful and disobedient; and remember that God will certainly requite it, if they have ever cursed their parents, even in their hearts, or have lifted up their hands against them, except they repent, and flee for refuge to the Saviour. And let parents hence learn to be very careful in training up their children, setting them a good example, especially in the government of their passions, and in praying for them; taking heed not to provoke them to wrath. Through poverty the Israelites sometimes sold themselves or their children; magistrates sold some persons for their crimes, and creditors were in some cases allowed to sell their debtors who could not pay. But “man-stealing,” the object of which is to force another into slavery, is ranked in the New Testament with the greatest crimes. Care is here taken, that satisfaction be made for hurt done to a person, though death do not follow. The gospel teaches masters to forbear, and to moderate threatenings, Eph 6:9, considering with Job, What shall I do, when God riseth up? Job 31:13, 14.
21.m:
God protects life through both his laws and his care. Someone who kills on purpose will face punishment, even if caught at God’s altar. However, God set up safe places called cities of refuge for people who accidentally caused someone else’s death while doing something legal. 

Children should listen to what God says about those who are mean and don’t obey their parents. Remember, God will punish anyone who has ever said bad things about their parents or hit them, unless they truly feel sorry and seek forgiveness. Parents need to teach their kids well, lead by example, control their anger, pray for them, and avoid making them angry.

Sometimes poor families had to sell themselves or their children. Magistrates would sell criminals, and creditors could sell debtors who couldn't repay debts. But kidnapping someone to make them a slave is one of the worst sins, according to the New Testament.

This section also talks about paying for harm done to someone, even if that person did not die. The gospel tells masters to be patient and not use harsh threats. Think about Job’s words: "What will happen when God stands up?" Job 31:13, 14.

21.o: The cases here mentioned give rules of justice then, and still in use, for deciding similar matters. We are taught by these laws, that we must be very careful to do no wrong, either directly or indirectly. If we have done wrong, we must be very willing to make it good, and be desirous that nobody may lose by us.
21.m: These examples teach rules of fairness that were used back then and are still used today. They show us that we should be very careful not to harm others, whether directly or indirectly. If we have caused harm, we need to be ready to fix things and make sure no one loses because of us.

23.o: In the law of Moses are very plain marks of sound moral feeling, and of true political wisdom. Every thing in it is suited to the desired and avowed object, the worship of one only God, and the separation of Israel from the pagan world. Neither parties, friends, witnesses, nor common opinions, must move us to lessen great faults, to aggravate small ones, excuse offenders, accuse the innocent, or misrepresent any thing.
23.m: The laws in the book of Moses show good moral values and smart leadership. Everything fits the goal of worshipping one God and keeping Israel separate from other nations. We shouldn’t let friendships, opinions, or anything else affect how we judge people. Don’t downplay big mistakes, exaggerate little ones, defend wrongdoers, blame innocents, or twist facts.

23.o: Every seventh year the land was to rest. They must not plough or sow it; what the earth produced of itself, should be eaten, and not laid up. This law seems to have been intended to teach dependence on Providence, and God's faithfulness in sending the larger increase while they kept his appointments. It was also typical of the heavenly rest, when all earthly labours, cares, and interests shall cease for ever. All respect to the gods of the heathen is strictly forbidden. Since idolatry was a sin to which the Israelites leaned, they must blot out the remembrance of the gods of the heathen. Solemn religious attendance on God, in the place which he should choose, is strictly required. They must come together before the Lord. What a good Master do we serve, who has made it our duty to rejoice before him! Let us devote with pleasure to the service of God that portion of our time which he requires, and count his sabbaths and ordinances to be a feast unto our souls. They were not to come empty-handed; so now, we must not come to worship God empty-hearted; our souls must be filled with holy desires toward him, and dedications of ourselves to him; for with such sacrifices God is well pleased.
23.m:
Every seven years, the land had to rest. People could not plow or plant crops during those times. Whatever grew naturally from the soil could be eaten, but nothing could be stored away. 

This rule taught people to depend on God’s care and trust that He would provide enough food even if they did not work the fields. The extra produce in other years showed God’s faithfulness when His commands were followed.

The Sabbath year also pointed ahead to heaven, where all earthly worries will end forever.

Worshiping false gods was strictly banned. Because the Israelites often turned to these idols, they needed to erase all memories of them.

God commanded them to gather at the special place He chose for religious meetings. Everyone was supposed to attend. Serving God should bring joy. We should happily give Him the time He asks for, seeing His Sabbaths and rules as a delight to our spirits.

When attending these gatherings, people couldn’t just show up empty-handed. In the same way, we shouldn’t approach God without sincere hearts. Our souls need to be full of devotion and dedication to Him. God is pleased by such offerings.

23.o: It is here promised that they should be guided and kept in their way through the wilderness to the land of promise, Behold, I send an angel before thee, mine angel. The precept joined with this promise is, that they be obedient to this angel whom God would send before them. Christ is the Angel of Jehovah; this is plainly taught by St. Paul, 1Co 10:9. They should have a comfortable settlement in the land of Canaan. How reasonable are the conditions of this promise; that they should serve the only true God; not the gods of the nations, which are no gods at all. How rich are the particulars of this promise! The comfort of their food, the continuance of their health, the increase of their wealth, the prolonging their lives to old age. Thus hath godliness the promise of the life that now is. It is promised that they should subdue their enemies. Hosts of hornets made way for the hosts of Israel; such mean creatures can God use for chastising his people's enemies. In real kindness to the church, its enemies are subdued by little and little; thus we are kept on our guard, and in continual dependence on God. Corruptions are driven out of the hearts of God's people, not all at once, but by little and little. The precept with this promise is, that they should not make friendship with idolaters. Those that would keep from bad courses, must keep from bad company. It is dangerous to live in a bad neighbourhood; others' sins will be our snares. Our greatest danger is from those who would make us sin against God.
23.m:
Here is the promised guidance and protection through the wilderness to the land of promise. God said, "I am sending my angel ahead of you." The command was to obey this angel. Jesus is called the Angel of the Lord; Saint Paul teaches this clearly in 1 Corinthians 10:9. 

They were promised a good home in the land of Canaan. To receive this blessing, they needed to worship only the one true God, not false gods. This promise included many benefits: enjoying their food, staying healthy, growing wealthy, living long lives. So, being religious brings blessings even in this life.

God also promised they would defeat their enemies. He used small insects like hornets to help the Israelites win battles. God often uses small things to punish those who oppose His people. Evil habits leave the hearts of God’s followers bit by bit, not all at once. 

The rule was not to befriend those who worship idols. People avoid trouble by avoiding sinful friends. Living near sinners can lead us to do wrong too. Our biggest risk comes from those who might tempt us to disobey God.

24.o: A solemn covenant was made between God and Israel. Very solemn it was, typifying the covenant of grace between God and believers, through Christ. As soon as God separated to himself a peculiar people, he governed them by a written word, as he has done ever since. God's covenants and commands are so just in themselves, and so much for our good, that the more we think of them, and the more plainly and fully they are set before us, the more reason we may see to comply with them. The blood of the sacrifice was sprinkled on the altar, on the book, and on the people. Neither their persons, their moral obedience, nor religious services, would meet with acceptance from a holy God, except through the shedding and sprinkling’ of blood. Also the blessings granted unto them were all of mercy; and the Lord would deal with them in kindness. Thus the sinner, by faith in the blood of Christ, renders willing and acceptable obedience. (Ex 24:9-11)
24.m:
God made a very serious promise with Israel. This promise was like the one God makes with believers today, through Jesus.

As soon as God chose a special group of people, he gave them rules to follow, just like he still does now. 

God’s promises and instructions are fair and good for us. Thinking about them helps us see why we should obey them.

The blood from the animal sacrifice was put on the altar, the book of rules, and the people. Without the blood being shed, God wouldn’t accept their actions or worship.

All the good things God promised them came out of his kindness. He treated them gently because of the blood.

In the same way, sinners who believe in Jesus' blood become willing and acceptable to God. (Exodus 24:9-11)

24.o: The elders saw the God of Israel; they had some glimpse of his glory, though whatever they saw, it was something of which no image or picture could be made, yet enough to satisfy them that God was with them of a truth. Nothing is described but what was under his feet. The sapphires are the pavement under his feet; let us put all the wealth of this world under our feet, and not in our hearts. Thus the believer sees in the face of Jesus Christ, far clearer discoveries of the glorious justice and holiness of God, than ever he saw under terrifying convictions; and through the Saviour, holds communion with a holy God.
24.m:
The elders saw the God of Israel. They got a glimpse of His glory, even if what they saw couldn’t be drawn or painted. It was enough for them to know God was truly with them. 

Nothing else is described except what was under His feet. The sapphires were part of the pavement beneath Him. We should place all the riches of this world under our feet, not in our hearts. 

Believers see clearer views of God’s glorious justice and holiness by looking at Jesus’ face. This is much better than seeing these things during times of fear and doubt. Through Jesus, believers can have fellowship with a holy God.

24.o: A cloud covered the mount six days; a token of God's special presence there. Moses was sure that he who called him up would protect him. Even those glorious attributes of God which are most terrible to the wicked, the saints with humble reverence rejoice in. And through faith in the atoning Sacrifice, we hope for greater honour than Moses ever enjoyed on earth. Now we see through a glass darkly, but when he shall appear, then face to face. This vision of God will continue with equal, if not increasing brightness of joy; not for a few days only, but through eternity.
24.m: A cloud covered the mountain for six days. It showed that God was really there. Moses knew that God would keep him safe. The holy parts of God that scare bad people make good people happy. We hope for even more honor than Moses had by believing in Jesus' sacrifice. Right now, we only see things unclearly, but one day we'll see clearly face-to-face. That amazing view of God will last forever, not just for a few days.

25.o: God chose the people of Israel to be a peculiar people to himself, above all people, and he himself would be their King. He ordered a royal palace to be set up among them for himself, called a sanctuary, or holy place, or habitation. There he showed his presence among them. And because in the wilderness they dwelt in tents, this royal palace was ordered to be a tabernacle, that it might move with them. The people were to furnish Moses with the materials, by their own free will. The best use we can make of our worldly wealth, is to honour God with it in works of piety and charity. We should ask, not only, What must we do? but, What may we do for God? Whatever they gave, they must give it cheerfully, not grudgingly, for God loves a cheerful giver, 2Co 9:7. What is laid out in the service of God, we must reckon well bestowed; and whatsoever is done in God's service, must be done by his direction. (Ex 25:10-22)
25.m:
God chose the people of Israel to be special to him, more than any other group. He wanted to be their king. So he told them to build a special house for him, which they called a sanctuary or holy place. 

In this house, God showed his presence to them. Since they lived in tents while traveling through the desert, God asked them to build a portable house, called a tabernacle, so it could move with them.

The people were supposed to provide Moses with the materials freely. They should use their money wisely by giving it to help God’s work. People should always wonder what they can do for God. When they give, they need to do it happily, not reluctantly. God likes happy givers (2 Corinthians 9:7).

Whatever they spent on God’s work, they should see it as well used. Everything done for God’s sake needs to follow his instructions. (Exodus 25:10-22)

25.o: The ark was a chest, overlaid with gold, in which the two tables of the law were to be kept. These tables are called the testimony; God in them testified his will. This law was a testimony to the Israelites, to direct them in their duty, and would be a testimony against them, if they transgressed. This ark was placed in the holy of holies; the blood of the sacrifices was sprinkled, and the incense burned, before it, by the high priest; and above it appeared the visible glory, which was the symbol of the Divine presence. This was a type of Christ in his sinless nature, which saw no corruption, in personal union with his Divine nature, atoning for our sins against it, by his death. The cherubim of gold looked one towards another, and both looked downward toward the ark. It denotes the angels' attendance on the Redeemer, their readiness to do his will, their presence in the assemblies of saints, and their desire to look into the mysteries of the gospel. It was covered with a covering of gold, called the mercy-seat. God is said to dwell, or sit between the cherubim, on the mercy-seat. There he would give his law, and hear supplicants, as a prince on his throne.
25.m:
The ark was a chest covered in gold where the two tablets of the law were stored. These tablets are called the testimony because God used them to show His will. The law guided the Israelites about what they should do and served as proof against them if they disobeyed.

This ark was put inside the most sacred room. The high priest would sprinkle the blood from sacrifices and burn incense there. Above the ark, there was a bright light that showed where God’s presence was. All of these things pointed to Jesus, who lived without sin while joined to His divine nature. He died to forgive us for breaking God’s laws.

Two golden figures called cherubs faced each other and looked down at the ark. They represent the angels ready to follow Jesus’ commands, present among believers, and eager to learn more about the truths of the gospel.

On top of the ark was a golden cover called the mercy seat. God was said to live or sit between the cherubs on the mercy seat. That’s where He gave His laws and listened to people praying, just like a king sitting on his throne.

25.o: A table was to be made of wood, overlaid with gold, to stand in the outer tabernacle, to be always furnished with the shew-bread. This table, with the articles on it, and its use, seems to typify the communion which the Lord holds with his redeemed people in his ordinances, the provisions of his house, the feasts they are favoured with. Also the food for their souls, which they always find when they hunger after it; and the delight he takes in their persons and services, as presented before him in Christ.
25.m:
A table made of wood covered with gold was placed in the outer part of the tabernacle. It was always filled with special bread called showbread. 

This table, along with what was on it, represents the relationship God has with his saved people during worship. It shows the things God provides for them and the meals they enjoy. 

It also stands for the spiritual food they always have when they feel hungry. And it shows how much joy God finds in their presence and service, offered through Christ.

25.o: The candlestick represents the light of God's word and Spirit, in and through Christ Jesus, afforded in this dark world to his believing people, to direct their worship and obedience, and to afford them consolations. The church is still dark, as the tabernacle was, in comparison with what it will be in heaven; but the word of God is a light shining in a dark place, 2Pe 1:19, and a dark place indeed the world would be without it. In ver. #(40) is an express caution to Moses. Nothing was left to his own fancy, or to that of the workmen, or the people; but the will of God must be observed in every particular. Christ's instruction to his disciples, Mt 28:20, is like this, Observe all things whatsoever I have commanded you. Let us remember that we are the temples of the Holy Ghost, that we have the law of God in our hearts, that we are to live a life of communion with God, feast on his ordinances, and are the light of the world, if indeed we are followers of Christ. May the Lord help us to try ourselves by this view of religion, and to walk according thereto.
25.m:
The candlestick stands for the light of God’s Word and Spirit, given to believers through Jesus in this dark world. It guides their worship and helps them obey God while giving them comfort too. 

Just like the old tabernacle, the church is still dark compared to what it will be in heaven. But God’s word shines brightly in this darkness, just like a light in a dark place. Without it, the world would be very dark indeed.

In verse 40, there’s a specific warning to Moses. He wasn’t allowed to use his imagination or let others decide. Instead, he had to follow God’s instructions exactly. This is similar to Jesus’ command to his followers in Matthew 28:20, where he tells them to do everything he has taught them.

We should remember that we are temples of the Holy Spirit. We carry God’s law in our hearts and need to live lives close to God, enjoying his teachings and being lights in the world if we truly follow him. 

May the Lord help us check ourselves against these ideas about true faith and live accordingly.

26.o: God manifested his presence among the Israelites in a tabernacle or tent, because of their condition in the wilderness. God suits the tokens of his favour, and the gifts of his grace, to his people's state and wants. The curtains of the tabernacle were to be very rich. They were to be embroidered with cherubim, signifying that the angels of God pitch their tents round about the church, Ps 34:7. (Ex 26:7-14)
26.m:
God showed he was with the Israelites by being present in a special tent while they were in the wilderness. He matched the signs of his favor and the blessings he gave them to fit their needs at the time.

The curtains for the tent were very fancy. They had pictures of angel faces sewn onto them. This reminded everyone that God’s angels protect his people, just like Psalm 34:7 says.

(Exodus 26:7-14)

26.o: The curtains of meaner materials, being made both longer and broader, covered the others, and were defended by coverings of skins. The whole represents the person and doctrine of Christ, and the church of true Christians, and all heavenly things, which outwardly are mean, but inwardly, and in the sight of God, are glorious and precious.
26.m:
The curtains made from simpler materials were longer and wider. They covered the other curtains and had coverings of animal skins for protection.

This describes Jesus, his teachings, and the group of true believers. On the outside, these might seem ordinary, but on the inside, they are beautiful and valuable in God’s eyes.

26.o: The sockets of silver each weighed about 115 pounds; they were placed in rows on the ground. In every pair of these sockets, a strong board of shittim-wood, covered with plates of gold, was fitted by mortises and tenons. Thus walls were formed for the two sides, and for the west end. The wall was further held together by bars, which passed through rings of gold. Over this the curtains were spread. Though movable, it was strong and firm. The materials were very costly. In all this it was a type of the church of God, built upon the foundation of the apostles and prophets, Jesus Christ himself being the chief Corner-stone, Eph 2:20, 21.
26.m:
Each socket made of silver weighed around 115 pounds and was laid out in rows on the ground. Two sockets held one thick wooden board covered with gold plates. These boards fit together tightly using joints called mortises and tenons. This created walls for both sides and the back end. 

Bars went through golden rings to keep the walls steady. Curtains then covered everything above the walls. Even though it could be moved, it was still strong and solid. All the materials used were very expensive.

This setup represented the church of God, which is built on the foundation of the apostles and prophets. Jesus Christ is the main cornerstone, as mentioned in Ephesians 2:20, 21.

26.o: A vail, or curtain, separated the holy place from the most holy place. It was hung upon pillars. This vail was for a partition between the holy place and the most holy; which forbade any to look into the holiest of all. The apostle tells what was the meaning of this vail, Heb 9:8. That the ceremonial law could not make the comers thereunto perfect, nor would the observance of it bring men to heaven; the way into the holiest of all was not made manifest, while the first tabernacle was standing. Life and immortality lay hidden till they were brought to light by the gospel; which was signified by the rending of this vail at the death of Christ, Mt 27:51. We have now boldness to enter into the holiest, in all acts of worship, by the blood of Jesus; yet such as obliges us to holy reverence. Another vail was for the outer door of the tabernacle. This vail was all the defence the tabernacle had. God takes care of his church on earth. A curtain shall be, if God please to make it so, as strong a defence to his house, as gates of brass and bars of iron. With this typical description of Christ and his church before us, what is our judgment of these matters? Do we see any glory in the person of Christ? any excellence in his character? any thing precious in his salvation? or any wisdom in the doctrine of the cross? Will our religion bear examination? and are we more careful to approve our hearts to God than our characters toward men?
26.m:
A curtain separated the holy place from the most holy place. It was hung on pillars. This curtain kept people from looking inside the most holy area. The writer explains its purpose in Hebrews 9:8. He says the old rules couldn’t make people perfect or save them. The way into the most holy place wasn’t shown until after Jesus died. 

Life and eternal life were hidden until revealed through the good news. This was shown by the curtain tearing when Jesus died, as in Matthew 27:51. Now we can go boldly into the presence of God in prayer and worship because of Jesus’ blood. But we still need to show respect.

There was another curtain for the entrance to the tent. This curtain protected the whole tent. God cares for his church. A curtain can protect God’s house just as well as strong metal doors. 

With these symbols of Jesus and his church in mind, what do you think? Can you see anything special about Jesus? Is there anything valuable in his teachings? Does the message of the cross make sense? Does your faith hold up under close inspection? Are you more concerned with pleasing God than impressing others?

27.o: In the court before the tabernacle, where the people attended, was an altar, to which they must bring their sacrifices, and on which their priests must offer them to God. It was of wood overlaid with brass. A grate of brass was let into the hollow of the altar, about the middle of which the fire was kept, and the sacrifice burnt. It was made of net-work like a sieve, and hung hollow, that the ashes might fall through. This brazen altar was a type of Christ dying to make atonement for our sins. The wood had been consumed by the fire from heaven, if it had not been secured by the brass: nor could the human nature of Christ have borne the wrath of God, if it had not been supported by Divine power.
27.m:
In front of the tabernacle, where people came to worship, there was an altar. They brought their sacrifices here and the priests offered them to God. 

The altar was made of wood covered with bronze. Inside the hollow part of the altar, there was a metal grid. The fire burned around the middle of the grid, and sacrifices were placed there to burn. The grid looked like a sieve, so ashes would fall through it.

This bronze altar represented Jesus, who died to forgive our sins. Without the bronze protecting the wood, the fire from heaven would have destroyed it. In the same way, without divine power supporting him, Jesus’s human body couldn’t have endured God’s anger.

27.o: The tabernacle was enclosed in a court, about sixty yards long and thirty broad, formed by curtains hung upon brazen pillars, fixed in brazen sockets. Within this enclosure the priests and Levites offered the sacrifices, and thither the Jewish people were admitted. These distinctions represented the difference between the visible nominal church, and the true spiritual church, which alone has access to God, and communion with him.
27.m:
The tabernacle had a courtyard around it that was about sixty yards long and thirty yards wide. The courtyard was made of curtains hanging on bronze poles stuck into bronze bases. Inside this area, the priests and Levites would offer sacrifices. Regular Jewish people could enter there too.

These differences showed the contrast between the outward church that everyone could see and the real inner group of believers who truly had access to God and could have fellowship with him.

27.o: The pure oil signified the gifts and graces of the Spirit, which all believers receive from Christ, the good Olive, and without which our light cannot shine before men. The priests were to light the lamps, and tend them. It is the work of ministers, by preaching and expounding the Scriptures, which are as a lamp, to enlighten the church, God's tabernacle upon earth. Blessed be God, this light is not now confined to the Jewish tabernacle, but is a light to lighten the gentiles, and for salvation unto the ends of the earth.
27.m:
The pure oil represents the gifts and grace we get from the Holy Spirit. All believers receive these gifts from Jesus, who is like the good olive tree. Without these gifts, our light cannot shine for others to see.

It was the job of the priests to light the lamps and take care of them. Today, it’s the role of ministers to teach people about God through preaching and explaining the Bible. They help spread God’s truth, just like lighting up a dark room.

Thankfully, this light is no longer limited to the Jewish temple. Now, it shines on everyone, including non-Jews around the world. This light brings salvation to all corners of the earth.

28.o: Hitherto the heads of families were the priests, and offered sacrifices; but now this office was confined to the family of Aaron only; and so continued till the gospel dispensation. The holy garments not only distinguished the priests from the people, but were emblems of that holy conduct which should ever be the glory and beauty, the mark of the ministers of religion, without which their persons and ministrations will be had in contempt. They also typified the glory of the Divine majesty, and the beauty of complete holiness, which rendered Jesus Christ the great High Priest. But our adorning under the gospel, is not to be of gold and costly array, but the garments of salvation, the robe of righteousness.
28.m:
Until now, the heads of families served as priests and offered sacrifices. But now, only those from the family of Aaron could do this job. This lasted until the time of the gospel.

The special clothes set the priests apart from regular people. These clothes showed that the priests needed to have good behavior all the time. Without this, people would think less of them. 

These clothes also reminded people about God’s perfect holiness and glory. They pointed to Jesus Christ, who was the greatest High Priest.

But today, we don’t need fancy clothes for church work. Instead, we wear the “garments” of salvation and righteousness.

28.o: This richly-wrought ephod was the outmost garment of the high priest; plain linen ephods were worn by the inferior priests. It was a short coat without sleeves, fastened close to the body with a girdle. The shoulder-pieces were buttoned together with precious stones set in gold, one on each shoulder, on which were engraven the names of the children of Israel. Thus Christ, our High Priest, presents his people before the Lord for a memorial. As Christ's coat had no seam, but was woven from the top throughout, so it was with the ephod. The golden bells on this ephod, by their preciousness and pleasant sound, well represent the good profession that the saints make, and the pomegranates the fruit they bring forth.
28.m:
The richly-made ephod was the outer garment of the high priest. Other priests wore simpler linen ephods. 

It was a short coat without sleeves, held close to the body with a belt. 

Stones set in gold buttons joined the shoulder pieces. One stone on each shoulder showed the names of the children of Israel. This shows how Jesus, our High Priest, brings his people before God as a reminder.

Like Jesus' seamless robe, the ephod was made whole from top to bottom. 

Golden bells on the ephod symbolize the faithful promises believers make. Pomegranates hanging with them show the good works they do.

28.o: The chief ornament of the high priest, was the breastplate, a rich piece of cloth, curiously worked. The name of each tribe was graven in a precious stone, fixed in the breastplate, to signify how precious, in God's sight, believers are, and how honourable. How small and poor soever the tribe was, it was as a precious stone in the breastplate of the high priest; thus are all the saints dear to Christ, however men esteem them. The high priest had the names of the tribes, both on his shoulders and on his breast, which reminds us of the power and the love with which our Lord Jesus pleads for those that are his. He not only bears them up in his arms with almighty strength, but he carries them in his bosom with tender affection. What comfort is this to us in all our addresses to God! The Urim and Thummim, by which the will of God was made known in doubtful cases, were put in this breastplate. Urim and Thummim signify light and integrity. There are many conjectures what these were; the most probable opinion seems to be, that they were the twelve precious stones in the high priest's breastplate. Now, Christ is our Oracle. By him God, in these last days, makes known himself and his mind to us, Heb 1:1, 2; Joh 1:18. He is the true Light, the faithful Witness, the Truth itself, and from him we receive the Spirit of Truth, who leads into all truth.
28.m:
The main decoration of the high priest was the breastplate, a beautiful piece of cloth with intricate designs. Each tribe’s name was carved onto a valuable gemstone attached to the breastplate. This showed how precious believers are to God and how honored they are.

Even if a tribe was small or poor, its name was still placed on a precious stone in the high priest’s breastplate. All the saints are dear to Christ, no matter how others may view them.

The high priest wore the names of the tribes both on his shoulders and on his chest. This reminded people about the power and love with which Jesus pleads for His followers. Not only does He lift them up with great strength, but He holds them close with tender care. 

This gives us comfort when we pray to God. Inside the breastplate were the Urim and Thummim, tools used to know God’s will in uncertain situations. These terms mean “light” and “integrity.” Many guess what these items were, but likely they were the twelve precious stones in the high priest’s breastplate.

Today, Jesus is our guide. Through Him, God reveals Himself and His plans to us, as seen in Hebrews 1:1, 2 and John 1:18. Jesus is the true Light, the honest Witness, and the ultimate Truth. We receive the Holy Spirit through Him, who guides us into all truth.

28.o: The robe of the ephod was under the ephod, and reached down to the knees, without sleeves. Aaron must minister in the garments appointed. We must serve the Lord with holy fear, as those who know they deserve to die. A golden plate was fixed on Aaron's forehead, engraven with “Holiness to the Lord.” Aaron was hereby reminded that God is holy, and that his priests must be holy, devoted to the Lord. This must appear in their forehead, in open profession of their relation to God. It must be engraven like the engravings of a signet; deep and durable; not painted so as to be washed off, but firm and lasting; such must our holiness to the Lord be. Christ is our High Priest; through him sins are forgiven to us, and not laid to our charge. Our persons, our doings, are pleasing to God upon the account of Christ, and not otherwise.
28.m:
Aaron wore a robe under the ephod that went down to his knees and had no sleeves. He had to wear these special clothes while serving God. When we serve God, we should do it with respect because we know we deserve punishment. 

A gold plate was attached to Aaron’s forehead with the words “Holy to the Lord” engraved on it. This reminded Aaron that God is holy and that he needed to be holy too. The words were carved deeply, like a stamp, to stay permanently. They showed that Aaron belonged to God.

This shows us that our devotion to God needs to be strong and lasting, just like the engraving on the plate. Jesus is our High Priest. Through him, our sins are forgiven, and God sees us as clean and acceptable. Because of Jesus, God accepts us and what we do.

28.o: The priest's garments typify the righteousness of Christ. If we appear not before God in that, we shall bear our iniquity, and die. Blessed is he, therefore, that watcheth, and keepeth his garments, Re 16:15. And blessed be God that we have a High Priest, appointed of God, and set apart for his work; furnished for his high office by the glory of his Divine majesty, and the beauty of perfect holiness. Happy are we, if by the law spiritually understood, we see that such a High Priest became us; that we cannot draw near to a holy God, or be accepted, but by him. There is no light, no wisdom, no perfection, but from him; no glory, no beauty, but in being like unto him. Let us take encouragement from the power, love, and compassion of our High Priest, to draw near with boldness to the throne of grace, that we may obtain mercy, and find grace to help in time of need.
28.m:
The priest’s clothes show the righteousness of Christ. If we don’t wear that righteousness before God, we will face punishment and death. So, it’s good that we stay alert and guard what we wear, Revelation 16:15. We are lucky to have a High Priest chosen by God and made ready for his duties. He has the honor of God’s greatness and the beauty of perfect holiness. It’s wonderful if we realize through God’s spiritual law that we need such a High Priest to come close to God and be accepted. 

There is no brightness, no wisdom, no perfection without him. Nothing brings glory or beauty except being like him. Let’s feel encouraged by the strength, love, and kindness of our High Priest to approach God boldly at his throne of grace. This way, we can receive mercy and get help when we need it most.

29.o: Aaron and his sons were to be set apart for the priest's office, with ceremony and solemnity. Our Lord Jesus is the great High Priest of our profession, called of God to be so; anointed with the Spirit, whence he is called Messiah, the Christ; clothed with glory and beauty; sanctified by his own blood; made perfect, or consecrated through sufferings, Heb 2:10. All believers are spiritual priests, to offer spiritual sacrifices, 1Pe 2:5, washed in the blood of Christ, and so made to our God priests, Re 1:5, 6. They also are clothed with the beauty of holiness, and have received the anointing, 1Jo 2:27. The Spirit of God is called the finger of God, (Lu 11:20, compared with Mt 12:28,) and by him the merit of Christ is applied to our souls. This consecration signifies the admission of a sinner into the spiritual priesthood, to offer spiritual sacrifices, acceptable to God through Jesus Christ.
29.m:
Aaron and his sons were chosen to become priests with special ceremonies. Jesus is our main priest, chosen by God to serve us. He was filled with the Holy Spirit, which is why he’s called the Messiah or Christ. He wears glory and beauty, and was made holy by his own blood. Through suffering, he became perfect, as Hebrews 2:10 tells us.

All believers are now spiritual priests who offer spiritual gifts to God. We’re washed clean by the blood of Jesus and made into priests for God, as Revelation 1:5 and 1 Peter 2:5 tell us. We wear the beauty of being holy and have been given the Holy Spirit, as John 1:27 explains.

The Holy Spirit is sometimes called the power of God, like in Luke 11:20 and Matthew 12:28. It brings the benefits of Jesus’ sacrifice to our lives. Being made into a spiritual priest means we can now offer good things to God through Jesus.

29.o: A lamb was to be offered upon the altar every morning, and a lamb every evening. This typified the continual intercession which Christ ever lives to make for his church. Though he offered himself but once for all, that one offering thus becomes a continual offering. This also teaches us to offer to God the spiritual sacrifices of prayer and praise every day, morning and evening. Our daily devotions are the most needful of our daily works, and the most pleasant of our daily comforts. Prayer-time must be kept up as duly as meal-time. Those starve their own souls, who keep not up constant attendance on the throne of grace; constancy in religion brings in the comfort of it.
29.m:
Every morning and every evening, a lamb was offered on the altar. This symbolizes the ongoing prayers that Jesus makes for his followers. Even though he made one sacrifice for everyone, its effect continues forever. 

This also shows us to pray and praise God each day, both in the morning and at night. Daily worship is very important and gives us great joy. We should treat prayer times just as carefully as we do mealtimes. People miss out on the blessings of faith if they don’t keep up regular communication with God through prayer. Consistency in religious practices leads to greater happiness from them.

30.o: The altar of incense represented the Son of God in his human nature, and the incense burned thereon typified his pleading for his people. The continual intercession of Christ was represented by the daily burning of incense thereon, morning and evening. Once every year the blood of the atonement was to be applied to it, denoting that the intercession of Christ has all its virtue from his sufferings on earth, and that we need no other sacrifice or intercessor but Christ alone.
30.m: The altar of incense stood for Jesus in his human form, and the burning incense showed him praying for his followers. The regular burning of incense each day, morning and night, showed how Jesus always pleads for us. Each year, the blood from the sacrifice had to be put on the altar, showing that Jesus' work on Earth gives power to his prayers. We don't need anyone else to pray for us except Jesus himself.

30.o: The tribute was half a shekel, about fifteen pence of our money. The rich were not to give more, nor the poor less; the souls of the rich and poor are alike precious, and God is no respecter of persons, Ac 10:34; Job 34:19. In other offerings men were to give according to their wordly ability; but this, which was the ransom of the soul, must be alike for all. The souls of all are of equal value, equally in danger, and all equally need a ransom. The money raised was to be used in the service of the tabernacle. Those who have the benefit, must not grudge the necessary charges of God's public worship. Money cannot make atonement for the soul, but it may be used for the honour of Him who has made the atonement, and for the maintenance of the gospel by which the atonement is applied.
30.m:
The tax was set at half a shekel, which is about fifteen pence in today’s money. Rich people couldn’t pay more than that, and poor people couldn’t pay less. Both rich and poor souls are equally valuable, and God treats everyone fairly, Acts 10:34; Job 34:19. 

For other gifts, people could give based on what they had. But for this special offering to save the soul, everyone paid the same amount. All souls are worth the same, all are in danger, and all need saving. 

The money collected went toward taking care of the tabernacle. People should help cover the costs of God’s house of worship if they use its services. 

Money can't forgive sins, but it can honor God and support the good work of spreading the gospel message.

30.o: A large vessel of brass, holding water, was to be set near the door of the tabernacle. Aaron and his sons must wash their hands and feet at this laver, every time they went in to minister. This was to teach them purity in all their services, and to dread the pollution of sin. They must not only wash and be made clean, when first made priests, but must wash and be kept clean, whenever they went to minister. It teaches us daily to attend upon God, daily to renew our repentance for sin, and our looking to the blood of Christ for remission; for in many things we daily offend.
30.m:
There was a big bronze basin filled with water placed close to the entrance of the tabernacle. Aaron and his sons had to wash their hands and feet each time they entered to do their duties. 

This taught them to keep themselves pure while serving God. They needed to avoid being polluted by sin. When they became priests, they washed once to become clean. But after that, they had to wash again before each service to stay clean.

It reminds us to meet with God every day. We should ask for forgiveness for our sins every day too. And we need to look to Jesus’ blood for cleansing because we make mistakes daily.

30.o: Directions are here given for making the holy anointing oil, and the incense to be used in the service of the tabernacle. To show the excellency of holiness, there was this spiced oil in the tabernacle, which was grateful to the sight and to the smell. Christ's name is as ointment poured forth, So 1:3, and the good name of Christians is like precious ointment, Ec 7:1. The incense burned upon the golden altar was prepared of sweet spices. When it was used, it was to be beaten very small; thus it pleased the Lord to bruise the Redeemer, when he offered himself for a sacrifice of a sweet-smelling savour. The like should not be made for any common use. Thus God would keep in the people's minds reverence for his own services, and teach us not to profane or abuse any thing whereby God makes himself known. It is a great affront to God to jest with sacred things, and to make sport with his word and ordinances. It is most dangerous and fatal to use professions of the gospel of Christ to forward wordly interests.
30.m:
Here are instructions for making the special oils and incense used in the tabernacle. These fragrant oils showed how valuable holiness is. They were pleasant to see and smell. Jesus' name is like perfume, Song of Solomon 1:3, and the good names of Christians are like precious perfumes, Ecclesiastes 7:1. 

The incense burned on the gold altar was made from sweet spices. When it was used, it had to be ground very fine. This reminds us of how God allowed the Saviour to suffer, offering himself as a pleasing sacrifice. 

No one could make these oils or incense for personal use. This taught the people to respect God’s worship and not misuse anything connected to him. It is wrong to joke about religious things or treat them lightly. Using the message of Jesus for selfish reasons is very dangerous.

31.o: The Israelites, who had been masons and bricklayers in Egypt, were not qualified for curious workmanship; but the Spirit who gave the apostles utterance in divers tongues, miraculously gave Bezaleel and Aholiab the skill that was wanting. The honour which comes from God, is always attended with a work to be done; to be employed for God is high honour. Those whom God calls to any service, he will find or make fit for it. The Lord gives different gifts to different persons; let each mind his proper work, diligently remembering that whatever wisdom any one possesses, the Lord put it in the heart, to do his commandments.
31.m:
The Israelites, who had worked as masons and bricklayers in Egypt, did not have the skills needed for fine craftsmanship. But God gave Bezaleel and Aholiab the special abilities they lacked. When God honors someone, He always gives them a task to complete. Being used by God is a great honor. God makes sure people have what they need to do His work. 

God gives different gifts to different people. Each person should focus on their own tasks. Remember that all the wisdom we have comes from God. He puts it in our hearts to follow His commands.

31.o: Orders were now given that a tabernacle should be set up for the service of God. But they must not think that the nature of the work, and the haste that was required, would justify them in working at it on sabbath days. The Hebrew word /shabath/ signifies rest, or ceasing from labour. The thing signified by the sabbath is that rest in glory which remains for the people of God; therefore the moral obligation of the sabbath must continue, till time is swallowed up in eternity.
31.m:
Orders were given to set up a tabernacle for worshipping God. But they shouldn’t think that their work was so important that they could skip resting on the Sabbath day. 

The Hebrew word “shabath” means rest or stopping work. The Sabbath points to the eternal rest that God’s people will have someday. So the rule about keeping the Sabbath must stay in place until we reach that final rest.

31.o: The law was written in tables of stone, to show how lasting it is: to denote likewise the hardness of our hearts; one might more easily write on stone, than write any thing good on our corrupt natural hearts. It was written with the finger of God; by his will and power. God only can write his law in the heart: he gives a heart of flesh; then, by his Spirit, which is the finger of God, writes his will in the heart, 2Co 3:3.
31.m:
The law was written on stones to show how permanent it is. This also shows how hard our hearts are—writing on stone is easier than writing anything good on our naturally bad hearts.

God wrote the law with his finger, showing it was done by his will and power. Only God can write his law in our hearts. He first gives us a heart that can feel and think well. Then, using his Spirit—which is like God’s finger—he writes his laws inside our hearts, as in 2 Corinthians 3:3.

32.o: While Moses was in the mount, receiving the law from God, the people made a tumultuous address to Aaron. This giddy multitude were weary of waiting for the return of Moses. Weariness in waiting betrays to many temptations. The Lord must be waited for till he comes, and waited for though he tarry. Let their readiness to part with their ear-rings to make an idol, shame our niggardliness in the service of the true God. They did not draw back on account of the cost of their idolatry; and shall we grudge the expenses of religion? Aaron produced the shape of an ox or calf, giving it some finish with a graving tool. They offered sacrifice to this idol. Having set up an image before them, and so changed the truth of God into a lie, their sacrifices were abomination. Had they not, only a few days before, in this very place, heard the voice of the Lord God speaking to them out of the midst of the fire, Thou shalt not make to thyself any graven image? Had they not themselves solemnly entered into covenant with God, that they would do all he had said to them, and would be obedient? ch. 24:7. Yet before they stirred from the place where this covenant had been solemnly made, they brake an express command, in defiance of an express threatening. It plainly shows, that the law was no more able to make holy, than it was to justify; by it is the knowledge of sin, but not the cure of sin. Aaron was set apart by the Divine appointment to the office of the priesthood; but he, who had once shamed himself so far as to build an altar to a golden calf, must own himself unworthy of the honour of attending at the altar of God, and indebted to free grace alone for it. Thus pride and boasting were silenced.
32.m:
When Moses was on the mountain getting laws from God, the people got restless and asked Aaron for help. They were tired of waiting for Moses to come back. Being impatient makes people do bad things. We should wait for God even if it takes a long time.

The people gave Aaron their earrings to make an idol. They weren’t stingy about what they used for their false god. Shouldn't we give generously to serve the real God?

Aaron shaped the gold into a calf figure using tools. Then they started offering sacrifices to this fake god. After setting up this statue, they turned away from God’s truth. Their offerings became sinful.

Just days earlier, hadn’t they heard God speak from the burning bush saying “Don’t make idols”? And hadn’t they promised to obey him? But right after making that promise, they broke one of his rules. 

This shows that following the law doesn’t make us good. Knowing the law helps us see our sins, but it can’t fix them. Even Aaron, chosen by God to be a priest, sinned badly here. He shouldn’t have built an altar to a golden calf. Only God’s mercy could forgive such a big mistake. Pride and bragging were put in their place.

32.o: God says to Moses, that the Israelites had corrupted themselves. Sin is the corruption of the sinner, and it is a self-corruption; every man is tempted when he is drawn aside of his own lust. They had turned aside out of the way. Sin is a departing from the way of duty into a by-path. They soon forgot God's works. He sees what they cannot discover, nor is any wickedness of the world hid from him. We could not bear to see the thousandth part of that evil which God sees every day. God expresses the greatness of his just displeasure, after the manner of men who would have prayer of Moses could save them from ruin; thus he was a type of Christ, by whose mediation alone, God would reconcile the world to himself. Moses pleads God's glory. The glorifying God's name, as it ought to be our first petition, and it is so in the Lord's prayer, so it ought to be our great plea. And God's promises are to be our pleas in prayer; for what he has promised he is able to perform. See the power of prayer. In answer to the prayers of Moses, God showed his purpose of sparing the people, as he had before seemed determined on their destruction; which change of the outward discovery of his purpose, is called repenting of the evil.
32.m:
God told Moses that the Israelites had corrupted themselves. Sin corrupts us, and we bring sin upon ourselves. People are tempted when they follow their desires. The Israelites had strayed from the right path. Sin leads us away from doing what’s right and onto wrong paths. 

They quickly forgot about God’s miracles. God knows everything, even things we don’t notice. We couldn’t handle seeing all the bad things God sees daily. God expressed His anger strongly, like an angry person might do. But God said that Moses’ prayers could save them from punishment. This made Moses a symbol of Jesus, who helps us get back to God.

Moses asked God to honor His name. Praising God should be our main request, as it is in the Lord’s Prayer. We should use God’s promises as reasons to pray because He always keeps His word. Prayer really works. Because of Moses' prayers, God decided to spare the people instead of destroying them. When God changes His mind about punishing someone, it’s called “repenting” of the harm.

32.o: What a change it is, to come down from the mount of communion with God, to converse with a wicked world. In God we see nothing but what is pure and pleasing; in the world nothing but what is sinful and provoking. That it might appear an idol is nothing in the world, Moses ground the calf to dust. Mixing this powder with their drink, signified that the backslider in heart should be filled with his own ways.
32.m:
Coming down from the mountain where God spoke to him was a big change for Moses. On the mountain, he only saw good things. But in the world below, there were many bad things.

To show that the golden calf was worthless, Moses crushed it into dust. He mixed the dust with water and made people drink it. This showed that those who turn away from God will face the consequences of their actions.

32.o: Never did any wise man make a more frivolous and foolish excuse than that of Aaron. We must never be drawn into sin by any thing man can say or do to us; for men can but tempt us to sin, they cannot force us. The approach of Moses turned the dancing into trembling. They were exposed to shame by their sin. The course Moses took to roll away this reproach, was, not by concealing the sin, or putting any false colour upon it, but by punishing it. The Levites were to slay the ringleaders in this wickedness; yet none were executed but those who openly stood forth. Those are marked for ruin who persist in sin: those who in the morning were shouting and dancing, before night were dying. Such sudden changes do the judgments of the Lord sometimes make with sinners that are secure and jovial in their sin.
32.m:
No one ever made a sillier excuse than what Aaron gave. Wise people should never let others talk them into doing wrong things. People can only try to get you to sin—they can’t force you.

When Moses came near, the dancers got scared. Their sin showed everyone how bad they looked. To fix the problem, Moses didn’t hide the truth or lie about what happened. He punished the sin instead.

The Levites had to kill the leaders of this wrongdoing. But only those who openly joined in were put to death. People who keep on sinning often face trouble. In the morning, some were cheering and dancing, but by nightfall, many were dead. Sometimes God’s punishments come quickly to those who think they’re safe and happy because of their sins.

32.o: Moses calls it a great sin. The work of ministers is to show people the greatness of their sins. The great evil of sin appears in the price of pardon. Moses pleads with God for mercy; he came not to make excuses, but to make atonement. We are not to suppose that Moses means that he would be willing to perish for ever, for the people's sake. We are to love our neighbour as ourselves, and not more than ourselves. But having that mind which was in Christ, he was willing to lay down his life in the most painful manner, if he might thereby preserve the people. Moses could not wholly turn away the wrath of God; which shows that the law of Moses was not able to reconcile men to God, and to perfect our peace with him. In Christ alone, God so pardons sin as to remember it no more. From this history we see, that no unhumbled, carnal heart, can long endure the holy precepts, the humbling truths, and the spiritual worship of God. But a god, a priest, a worship, a doctrine, and a sacrifice, suited to the carnal mind, will ever meet with abundance of worshippers. The very gospel itself may be so perverted as to suit a worldly taste. Well is it for us, that the Prophet like unto Moses, but who is beyond compare more powerful and merciful, has made atonement for our souls, and now intercedes in our behalf. Let us rejoice in his grace.
32.m:
Moses calls it a big sin. A minister’s job is to help people see just how bad their sins are. The real harm of sin becomes clear when you look at what it takes to forgive someone.

Moses asks God for mercy. He didn’t try to excuse himself, but instead tried to make things right. We shouldn't think Moses wanted to suffer forever to save others. Instead, we should care about others as much as we do ourselves. Like Jesus, Moses was ready to go through pain to protect the people.

But even Moses couldn’t stop God’s anger completely. This shows that following the laws given by Moses wasn’t enough to bring true peace with God. Only Jesus can fully forgive sins and forget them.

From this story, we learn that hearts that aren’t humble and sincere can’t handle God’s rules, truths, and worship. People often prefer religions that fit their desires. Even the good news of the gospel can be twisted to match worldly tastes.

Thankfully, there is a better prophet than Moses—one far more powerful and kind—who makes full payment for our sins and now speaks up for us. Let’s be glad for his kindness.

33.o: Those whom God pardons, must be made to know what their sin deserved. “Let them go forward as they are;” this was very expressive of God's displeasure. Though he promises to make good his covenant with Abraham, in giving them Canaan, yet he denies them the tokens of his presence they had been blessed with. The people mourned for their sin. Of all the bitter fruits and consequences of sin, true penitents most lament, and dread most, God's departure from them. Canaan itself would be no pleasant land without the Lord's presence. Those who parted with ornaments to maintain sin, could do no less than lay aside ornaments, in token of sorrow and shame for it.
33.m: Those whom God forgives need to understand what their sins deserve. "Let them go forward as they are;" this showed God’s displeasure clearly. Even though He promised to keep His covenant with Abraham and give them Canaan, He took away the signs of His presence that they once enjoyed. The people felt sad about their sin. Among all the bad results of sin, those truly sorry for their actions fear most that God will leave them. Without God’s presence, even having Canaan wouldn’t be enjoyable. People who gave up their jewelry to pay for their sins should now take off more jewelry to show they feel sorry and ashamed.

33.o: Moses took the tabernacle, and pitched it without the camp. This seems to have been a temporary building, set up for worship, and at which he judged disputes among the people. The people looked after him; they were very desirous to be at peace with God, and concerned to know what would come to pass. The cloudy pillar which had withdrawn from the camp when it was polluted with idolatry, now returned. If our hearts go forth toward God to meet him, he will graciously come to meet us.
33.m:
Moses set up the tent of meeting outside the camp. It seemed to be a temporary place where people could pray and worship. Moses used this tent to settle disagreements between the people. 

The people watched Moses closely because they really wanted to stay on good terms with God. They cared about knowing what would happen next.

The cloud that had left the camp when the people worshipped idols came back. When we reach out to God with sincere hearts, he gladly comes to us.

33.o: Moses is very earnest with God. Thus, by the intercession of Christ, we are not only saved from ruin, but become entitled to everlasting happiness. Observe here how he pleads. We find grace in God's sight, if we find grace in our hearts to guide and quicken us in the way of our duty. Moses speaks as one who dreaded the thought of going forward without the Lord's presence. God's gracious promises, and mercy towards us, should not only encourage our faith, but also excite our fervency in prayer. Observe how he speeds. See, in a type, Christ's intercession, which he ever lives to make for all that come to God by him; and that it is not by any thing in those for whom he intercedes. Moses then entreats a sight of God's glory, and is heard in that also. A full discovery of the glory of God, would overwhelm even Moses himself. Man is mean, and unworthy of it; weak, and could not bear it; guilty, and could not but dread it. The merciful display which is made in Christ Jesus, alone can be borne by us. The Lord granted that which would abundantly satisfy. God's goodness is his glory; and he will have us to know him by the glory of his mercy, more than by the glory of his majesty. Upon the rock there was a fit place for Moses to view the goodness and glory of God. The rock in Horeb was typical of Christ the Rock; the Rock of refuge, salvation, and strength. Happy are they who stand upon this Rock. The cleft may be an emblem of Christ, as smitten, crucified, wounded, and slain. What follows, denotes the imperfect knowledge of God in the present state, even as revealed in Christ; for this, when compared with the heavenly sight of him. is but like seeing a man that is gone by, whose back only is to be seen. God in Christ, as he is, even the fullest and brightest displays of his glory, grace, and goodness, are reserved to another state.
33.m:
Moses really wanted God’s help. Through Christ’s prayers, we get saved and earn eternal joy. Notice how Moses asked. He found favor with God because he had kindness in his heart to do what was right. Moses spoke as someone afraid to move ahead without God’s guidance. God’s promises and mercy should strengthen our faith and make us pray harder. 

See how Moses’ plea worked. It shows us Christ’s ongoing work of praying for believers. And it’s not based on anything we did. Moses then begged to see God’s glory, and God listened. Even Moses couldn’t handle seeing all of God’s glory. Humans are too small, weak, and sinful. Only through Christ can we endure such a sight.

God showed Moses enough to fully satisfy him. His goodness is part of God’s glory. Knowing God’s mercy through Christ is better than knowing just his power. On the rock at Horeb, Moses saw God’s goodness and glory. This rock symbolizes Christ, our safe haven and source of salvation. Those standing on Christ are blessed.

The crack in the rock might represent Christ, wounded and dying. What followed was a limited glimpse of God through Christ. Compared to heaven, it’s like seeing someone walking away. In Christ, God reveals much about his glory, grace, and love, but we’ll see even more in the next life.

34.o: When God made man in his own image, the moral law was written in his heart, by the finger of God, without outward means. But since the covenant then made with man was broken, the Lord has used the ministry of men, both in writing the law in the Scriptures, and in writing it in the heart. When God was reconciled to the Israelites, he ordered the tables to be renewed, and wrote his law in them. Even under the gospel of peace by Christ, the moral law continues to bind believers. Though Christ has redeemed us from the curse of the law, yet not from the commands of it. The first and the best evidence of the pardon of sin, and peace with God, is the writing the law in the heart.
34.m:
When God created humans in his image, he put the moral law in their hearts directly. But after people broke the agreement with God, he had human helpers write down the law in books and teach it to others. 

Even when God forgave the Israelites, he gave them new stone tablets with the law written on them. Under Jesus' teachings, the moral law still applies to those who believe. Although Jesus freed us from the punishment for breaking the law, we're still expected to follow its rules.

The clearest sign that our sins are forgiven and we have peace with God is when the law is written in our hearts.

34.o: The Lord descended by some open token of his presence and manifestation of his glory in a cloud, and thence proclaimed his NAME; that is, the perfections and character which are denoted by the name JEHOVAH. The Lord God is merciful; ready to forgive the sinner, and to relieve the needy. Gracious; kind, and ready to bestow undeserved benefits. Long-suffering; slow to anger, giving time for repentance, only punishing when it is needful. He is abundant in goodness and truth; even sinners receive the riches of his bounty abundantly, though they abuse them. All he reveals is infallible truth, all he promises is in faithfulness. Keeping mercy for thousands; he continually shows mercy to sinners, and has treasures, which cannot be exhausted, to the end of time. Forgiving iniquity, and transgression, and sin; his mercy and goodness reach to the full and free forgiveness of sin. And will by no means clear the guilty; the holiness and justice of God are part of his goodness and love towards all his creatures. In Christ's sufferings, the Divine holiness and justice are fully shown, and the evil of sin is made known. God's forgiving mercy is always attended by his converting, sanctifying grace. None are pardoned but those who repent and forsake the allowed practice of every sin; nor shall any escape, who abuse, neglect, or despise this great salvation. Moses bowed down, and worshipped reverently. Every perfection in the name of God, the believer may plead with Him for the forgiveness of his sins, the making holy of his heart, and the enlargement of the Redeemer's kingdom.
34.m:
The Lord came down with a sign showing his presence and revealed his glory in a cloud. There, he declared his NAME, which tells us about his perfect nature and character, especially what the name JEHOVAH means.

God is merciful—he’s quick to forgive sinners and help those in need. He’s gracious—kind and willing to give gifts without needing anything in return. He’s long-suffering—slow to get angry, allowing time for people to turn from their wrong ways before punishing them if needed.

He is rich in goodness and truth. Even sinners receive many blessings, even if they misuse them. Everything God shares is true, and all his promises come true faithfully. He keeps showing mercy for generations, with endless supplies of kindness until the end of time.

He forgives all kinds of sins, mistakes, and wrongdoing. Yet, he won’t let guilty people go unpunished. His holiness and justice show his love for everyone. When Jesus suffered, it showed the true cost of sin and God’s desire to forgive.

When God forgives, he also gives new life and helps people become more holy. Only those who truly turn away from their sins are forgiven. Those who ignore or reject God’s offer of salvation won’t escape punishment.

Moses bowed down and worshiped respectfully. A believer can ask God for forgiveness, purity, and growth of the Redeemer’s kingdom using all these qualities in God’s name.

34.o: The Israelites are commanded to destroy every monument of idolatry, however curious or costly; to refuse all alliance, friendship, or marriage with idolaters, and all idolatrous feasts; and they were reminded not with idolaters, and all idolatrous feats; and they were reminded not to repeat the crime of making molten images. Jealously is called the rage of a man, Pr 6:34; but in God it is holy and just displeasure. Those cannot worship God aright, who do not worship him only.
34.m:
The Israelites were told to get rid of every sign of false gods, even if those signs were fancy or expensive. They should not form alliances, friendships, or marriages with people who worship false gods. They should avoid attending their religious festivals too. 

They were warned not to copy the mistake of creating statues of false gods. A person’s jealousy is often seen as anger, but God’s jealousy is pure and right. People cannot truly worship God unless they worship Him alone.

34.o: Once a week they must rest, even in ploughing time, and in harvest. All worldly business must give way to that holy rest; even harvest work will prosper the better, for the religious observance of the sabbath day in harvest time. We must show that we prefer our communion with God, and our duty to him, before the business or the joy of harvest. Thrice a year they must appear before the Lord God, the God of Israel. Canaan was a desirable land, and the neighbouring nations were greedy; yet God says, They shall not desire it. Let us check all sinful desires against God and his glory, in our hearts, and then trust him to check all sinful desires in the hearts of others against us. The way of duty is the way of safety. Those who venture for him never lose by him. Three feasts are here mentioned: 1. The Passover, in remembrance of the deliverance out of Egypt. 2. The feast of weeks, or the feast of Pentecost; added to it is the law of the first-fruits. 3. The feast of in-gathering, or the feast of Tabernacles. Moses is to write these words, that the people might know them better. We can never be enough thankful to God for the written word. God would make a covenant with Israel, in Moses as a mediator. Thus the covenant of grace is made with believers through Christ.
34.m:
They must take one day off each week, even during planting and harvesting times. All other tasks must wait until after they have rested on the Sabbath. Even the busy work of harvest goes better if they honor the day of rest. 

We should put our time with God ahead of our work or joy from the harvest. 

Three times a year, they had to go see the Lord God, the God of Israel. The land of Canaan was very good, and nearby nations wanted it. But God said they would not want it. We should stop any wrong desires about God’s things in our hearts, and trust Him to keep others from having bad thoughts toward us.

Following God’s rules keeps us safe. People who follow God do not lose anything.

There are three special times mentioned: 
1. The Passover, which reminds them of being freed from Egypt.
2. The Feast of Weeks, or Pentecost, along with the rule about offering the first fruits.
3. The Feast of Ingathering, or Tabernacles.

Moses wrote down these instructions so the people could learn them well. We should always be grateful for God giving us His Word.

God promised to make a covenant with Israel through Moses as their helper. In the same way, God makes a promise of love to believers through Jesus.

34.o: Near and spiritual communion with God improves the graces of a renewed and holy character. Serious godliness puts a lustre upon a man's countenance, such as commands esteem and affection. The vail which Moses put on, marked the obscurity of that dispensation, compared with the gospel dispensation of the New Testament. It was also an emblem of the natural vail on the hearts of men respecting spiritual things. Also the vail that was and is upon the nation of Israel, which can only be taken away by the Spirit of the Lord showing to them Christ, as the end of the law for righteousness to every one that believeth. Fear and unbelief would put the vail before us, they would hinder our free approach to the mercy-seat above. We should spread our wants, temporal and spiritual, fully before our heavenly Father; we should tell him our hinderances, struggles, trails, and temptations; we should acknowledge our offences.
34.m:
Being close to God and having a personal relationship with Him helps people become more kind and good. When someone is truly serious about their faith, it shows in their face, making others want to love and respect them.

Moses wore a veil over his face because the old way of worship wasn’t as bright as the new way taught by Jesus. This veil also showed that people couldn't see clearly about spiritual things until God helped them. 

The veil also represents how many Jewish people didn't fully understand who Jesus was until God revealed Him to them. Fear and doubt keep the veil up, stopping us from coming freely to God’s throne. We should share all our needs—both physical and spiritual—with our Heavenly Father. We should tell Him about our problems, struggles, and sins.

35.o: The mild and easy yoke of Christ has made our sabbath duties more delightful, and our sabbath restraints less irksome, than those of the Jews; but we are the more guilty by neglecting them. Surely God's wisdom in giving us the sabbath, with all the mercy of its purposes, are sinfully disregarded. Is it nothing to pour contempt upon the blessed day, which a bounteous God has given to us for our growth in grace with the church below, and to prepare us for happiness with the church above?
35.m:
The gentle rules of Christ have made our Sabbath duties more enjoyable and the restrictions easier to follow compared to what the Jews had. But we still feel guilty when we don't do them properly. 

God’s wise plan in giving us the Sabbath, along with all its merciful benefits, is often ignored by us. Isn’t it wrong to show disrespect toward such a special day that a generous God gave us? This day helps us grow spiritually within the church here on earth and prepares us for heaven.
