35.o: The tabernacle was to be dedicated to the honour of God, and used in his service; and therefore what was brought for it, was an offering to the Lord. The rule is, Whosoever is of a willing heart, let him bring. All that were skilful must work. God dispenses his gifts; and as every man hath received, so he must minister, 1Pe 4:10. Those that were rich, must bring in materials to work on; those that were skilful, must serve the tabernacle with their skill: as they needed one another, so the tabernacle needed them both, 1Co 12:7–21.
35.m:
The tabernacle was meant to honor God and be used for his service. So anything given for it was considered an offering to the Lord. 

The rule was: Whoever has a willing heart should bring their gift. People who had skills were asked to use them. God gives different gifts, and we should use whatever gift we have received, just like Peter said in 1 Peter 4:10.

Rich people could give materials. Skilled workers could offer their abilities. They needed each other, and the tabernacle needed all of them together, as Paul explained in 1 Corinthians 12:7-21.

35.o: Without a willing mind, costly offerings would be abhorred; with it, the smallest will be accepted. Our hearts are willing, when we cheerfully assist in promoting the cause of God. Those who are diligent and contented in employments considered mean, are as much accepted of God as those engaged in splendid services. The women who spun the goats' hair were wise-hearted, because they did it heartily to the Lord. Thus the labourer, mechanic, or servant who attends to his work in the faith and fear of God, may be as wise, for his place, as the most useful minister, and he equally accepted of the Lord. Our wisdom and duty consist in giving God the glory and use of our talents, be they many or few.
35.m:
Without a willing heart, even expensive gifts would be rejected. But with a willing heart, even small offerings are accepted by God.

Our hearts are willing when we joyfully help promote God’s work. People who work hard at jobs others might think are unimportant are just as valued by God as those doing grand tasks.

The women who spun goat hair showed wisdom because they did their work gladly for the Lord. So too, someone working as a laborer, craftsman, or servant can honor God by doing their job faithfully. They can be as valuable to God as ministers.

True wisdom and duty come from using whatever abilities we have—whether big or small—to give glory to God.

35.o: Here is the Divine appointment of the master-workmen, that there might be no strife for the office, and that all who were employed in the work might take direction from, and give account to them. Those whom God called by name to his service, he filled with the Spirit of God. Skill, even in worldly employments, is God's gift, and comes from above. But many are ready enough in cutting out work for other people, and can tell what this man or that man should do; but the burdens they bind on others, they themselves will not touch with one of their fingers. Such will fall under the character of slothful servants. These men were not only to devise and to work themselves, but they were to teach others. Those that rule should teach; and those to whom God has given knowledge, should be willing to make it known for the benefit of others.
35.m:
Here is where God chose the leaders for the project. This way, everyone knew who was in charge and could follow their directions. 

God picked certain people by name to serve him. He gave these chosen ones special abilities through the Holy Spirit. Even skills used in regular jobs come from God. 

Some people love telling others what to do, but don't want to do the hard work themselves. That kind of behavior makes someone lazy. 

These leaders had to plan and create things themselves. They also needed to teach others how to do it too. People who have power should share their knowledge so others can learn.

38.o: In all ages of the church there have been some persons more devoted to God, more constant in their attendance upon his ordinances, and more willing to part even with lawful things, for his sake, than others. Some women, devoted to God and zealous for the tabernacle worship, expressed zeal by parting with their mirrors, which were polished plates of brass. Before the invention of looking-glasses, these served the same purposes. (Ex 38:9-20)
38.m:
Throughout history, there have always been people who love God very much. They go to church regularly and are willing to give up even their favorite things for God’s sake. 

Some women who loved God deeply and cared about worshipping at the tabernacle showed their devotion by giving away their mirrors. These mirrors were made from polished metal because real glass mirrors hadn’t been invented yet. (Exodus 38:9-20)

38.o: The walls of the court being of curtains only, intimated that the state of the Jewish church itself was movable and changeable; and in due time to be taken down and folded up, when the place of the tent should be enlarged, and its cords lengthened, to make room for the Gentile world.
38.m: The walls of the court were made of curtains, showing that the state of the Jewish church could move and change. At the right time, these curtains would come down and get packed away. This was to make space for more people, including those from other nations, when the church grew larger.

38.o: The foundation of massy pieces of silver showed the solidity and purity of the truth upon which the church is founded. Let us regard the Lord Jesus Christ while reading of the furniture of the tabernacle. While looking at the altar of burnt-offering, let us see Jesus. In him, his righteousness, and salvation, is a full and sufficient offering for sin. In the laver of regeneration, by his Holy Spirit, let our souls be washed, and they shall be clean; and as the people offered willingly, so may our souls be made willing. Let us be ready to part with any thing, and count all but loss to win Christ.
38.m: The heavy pieces of silver show how solid and pure the truth is that the church is built on. When we read about the things inside the tabernacle, let’s think about Jesus. As we look at the altar where sacrifices were burned, let’s remember Jesus. He offers complete forgiveness through his righteousness and salvation. At the basin used for washing, let’s ask God’s Holy Spirit to cleanse our souls. Just as the people freely gave offerings, may our hearts be willing too. We should be ready to give up anything to follow Jesus.

39.o: The priests' garments were rich and splendid. The church in its infancy was thus taught by shadows of good things to come; but the substance is Christ, and the grace of the gospel. Christ is our great High Priest. When he undertook the work of our redemption, he put on the clothes of service, he arrayed himself with the gifts and graces of the Spirit, girded himself with resolution to go through the undertaking, took charge of all God's spiritual Israel, laid them near his heart, engraved them on the palms of his hands, and presented them to his Father. And he crowned himself with holiness to the Lord, consecrating his whole undertaking to the honour of his Father's holiness. True believers are spiritual priests. The clean linen with which all their clothes of service must be made, is the righteousness of saints, Re 19:8.
39.m:
The priests wore beautiful clothing. In the early days of the church, people learned about future blessings through these symbols. But the real blessing is Jesus and the grace found in the gospel.

Jesus is our main priest. When he started his work to save us, he dressed himself with the Holy Spirit’s gifts and strengths. He prepared himself with determination to complete his mission. He carried God’s chosen people close to his heart, wrote their names on his hands, and showed them to his Father. 

He dedicated everything he did to honor God’s holiness. People who truly believe in Jesus are like spiritual priests too. Their special clothes for serving are made from the righteousness of believers, as mentioned in Revelation 19:8.

39.o: The tabernacle was a type or emblem of Jesus Christ. As the Most High dwelt visibly within the sanctuary, even on the ark, so did he reside in the human nature and tabernacle of his dear Son; in Christ dwelt all the fulness of the Godhead bodily, Col 2:9. The tabernacle was a symbol of every real Christian. In the soul of every true follower of the Saviour the Father dwells, the object of his worship, and the author of his blessings. The tabernacle also typified the church of the Redeemer. The meanest and the mightiest are alike dear to the Father's love, freely exercised through faith in Christ. The tabernacle was a type and emblem of the heavenly temple, Re 21:3. What, then, will be the splendour of His appearance, when the cloud shall be withdrawn, and his faithful worshippers shall see him as he is!
39.m:
The tabernacle was a symbol of Jesus Christ. Just as the Most High lived inside the sanctuary above the ark, God lived within Jesus' human body. All the fullness of God lived in Christ, as we see in Colossians 2:9. 

The tabernacle also represented each true Christian. Inside the heart of every genuine believer, the Father lives. He is the one they worship and bless them.

Furthermore, the tabernacle stood for the church of Jesus. Both the humblest member and the most powerful one are equally loved by God because of their faith in Christ.

Lastly, the tabernacle pointed to the heavenly temple described in Revelation 21:3. When the time comes and the cloud is gone, what glory there will be! Then, those who faithfully follow him will see God as he truly is.

40.o: When a new year begins, we should seek to serve God better than the year before. In half a year the tabernacle was completed. When the hearts of numbers are earnest in a good cause, much may be done in a short time; and when the commandments of God are continually attended to, as the rule of working, all will be done well. The high-priesthood was in the family of Aaron till Christ came, and in Him, the substance of all these shadows, it continues for ever.
40.m:
At the start of each new year, we should aim to serve God even better than we did the previous year. 

The tabernacle was finished in just six months. When many people work hard on a good project, a lot can get done quickly. And when everyone follows God’s rules while doing their work, everything turns out great.

Before Jesus came, only Aaron’s family could be priests. But now, through Christ, true priesthood lasts forever.

40.o: When the tabernacle and the furniture of it were prepared, they did not put off rearing it till they came to Canaan; but, in obedience to the will of God, they set it up in the midst of their camp. Those who are unsettled in the world, must not think that this will excuse want of religion; as if it were enough to begin to serve God when they begin to be settled in the world. No; a tabernacle for God is very needful, even in a wilderness, especially as we may be in another world before we come to fix in this. And we may justly fear lest we should deceive ourselves with a form of godliness. The thought that so few entered Canaan, should warn young persons especially, not to put off the care of their souls.
40.m:
When the tabernacle and its furnishings were ready, they didn’t wait until they reached Canaan to set them up. They followed God’s instructions and built the tabernacle right there in their camp.

People who are moving around a lot shouldn’t use that as an excuse for not practicing their faith. It’s not okay to wait to start serving God until you’re settled somewhere. A place for God is important even in difficult times, including while traveling through life. We might enter the next world before finding stability here.

We should worry about fooling ourselves by only going through the motions of being religious. Thinking about how few people made it to Canaan should remind young people especially to take care of their souls now.

40.o: The cloud covered the tabernacle even in the clearest day; it was not a cloud which the sun scatters. This cloud was a token of God's presence to be seen day and night, by all Israel, that they might never again question, Is the Lord among us, or is he not? It guided the camp of Israel through the wilderness. While the cloud rested on the tabernacle, they rested; when it removed, they followed it. The glory of the Lord filled the tabernacle. In light and fire the Shechinah made itself visible: God is Light; our God is a consuming Fire. Yet so dazzling was the light, and so dreadful the fire, that Moses was not able to enter into the tent of the congregation, till the splendour was abated. But what Moses could not do, our Lord Jesus has done, whom God caused to draw near; and who has invited us to come boldly, even to the mercy-seat. Being taught by the Holy Spirit to follow the example of Christ, as well as to depend upon him, to attend his ordinances, and obey his precepts, we shall be kept from losing our way, and be led in the midst of the paths of judgment, till we come to heaven, the habitation of his holiness. BLESSED BE GOD FOR JESUS CHRIST!
40.m:
A cloud always covered the tabernacle, even on the brightest days. It wasn’t a cloud that the sun would scatter. This cloud showed God’s presence to everyone in Israel, both day and night. They could see it and know for sure if God was there or not.

The cloud guided them through the wilderness. When the cloud stayed over the tabernacle, the people stayed put. When it moved, they followed it.

God’s glory filled the tabernacle. He appeared in light and fire. God is Light, and our God is a consuming Fire. But the light was too bright, and the fire too scary, for Moses to go inside the tent until the brightness lessened.

Moses couldn’t enter the tent because of the light, but Jesus did. Jesus drew near to God and invites us to come boldly to the mercy seat. We should follow Jesus’ example and trust in him. We should also attend his teachings and obey his commands. Then we won’t get lost and will stay on the right path to heaven, where God’s holiness dwells.

BLESSED BE GOD FOR JESUS CHRIST!
